coredata.dc:title: Construction of novel Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 system with enhanced electron transfer capacity for visible light photocatalytic degradation of sulfanilamide
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ruxue
Tang, Jianhua
Zhang, Xingyuan
Wang, Di
Wang, Xuan
Xue, Shuang
Zhang, Zhaohong
Dionysiou, Dionysios D.
coredata.dc:description: A novel Z-scheme system, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 with enhanced electron transfer capacity was constructed for degrading sulfanilamide (SAM) using solar light. The photocatalytic activity of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 was investigated. The effects of the mass ratio (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4), doped V dose, Ag wt.% content, and irradiation time on the catalytic performance were evaluated. The reasonable mechanism of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 solar photocatalytic degradation was also presented. These results reveal Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 possesses enhanced photocatalytic performance. The loaded Ag as electron mediator increases the electron transfer rate. Particularly, the doped V and the Fe ions from ZnFe2O4 form a powerful electron driving force, which enhances the electron transfer capacity. Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 shows optimal photocatalytic performance at 2.0 wt.% Ag and 0.5% doped V dose (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4 = 1.0:0.5). Also, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 exhibits high stability and repeatability in photocatalytic degradation. Several active species (•OH, •O2 −, and h +) are produced in the Z-scheme photodegradation of SAM. These results on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 are ascribed to synergistic photocatalytic effects of ZnFe2O4 and BiTa1-xVxO4 mediated through Ag and driven by doped V and Fe ions. Therefore, the Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic technology proves to be promising for the solar photocatalytic treatment of antibiotics under solar light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electron transfer capacity
Photocatalytic
Visible light
Sulfanilamide
Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SEM, EDX, and TEM of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposites
Influence of mass ratio of ZnFe2O4 and BiTaO4 on photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4
Influence of dose of doped V on photocatalytic activity of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4
Influence of wt.% of Ag on photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 and ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4
Influence of irradiation time on photocatalytic performance of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4
Possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of ZnFe2O4 (a), BiTaO4 (b), BiTa1-xVxO4 (c), and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 (d).
SEM images of ZnFe2O4 (a), BiTaO4 (b), and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 (c).
TEM image (a, b) and HRTEM images (c, d) of as-prepared Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4.
FT-IR spectrum of ZnFe2O4 (a), BiTaO4 (b), BiTa1-xVxO4 (c), and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 (d).
XPS spectra of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4: survey spectrum (a), high-resolution spectra of Zn 2p (b), Fe 2p (c), Bi 4f (d), Ta 4f (e), V 2p (f), Ag 3d (g) and O 1 s (h).
(a) UV–vis DRS of ZnFe2O4, BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4 and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4; (b) plots of (αhυ)2 versus hυ for ZnFe2O4, BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4 and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4.
PL spectra of BiTaO4, ZnFe2O4, BiTa1-xVxO4 and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4. (λex = 365 nm).
Influence of mass ratio (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4) on photocatalytic activity of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 (10.0 mg/L SAM; 1.0 g/L dose; 4.0 h light).
Influence of dose of doped V on photocatalytic activity of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4 = 1.0:1.0; 10.0 mg/L SAM; 1.0 g/L dose of catalyst; 4.0 h light).
Influence of Ag on photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4 = 1.0:1.0; 10.0 mg/L SAM; 1.0 g/L dose; 4.0 h light).
Influence of Ag on photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4. (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4 = 1.0:0.5; 0.5% V; 10.0 mg/L SAM; 1.0 g/L dose of catalyst; 4.0 h light).
UV–vis absorption spectra of SAM solution on Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 (10.0 mg/L SAM, 1.0 g/L dose of catalyst).
Influence of (a) irradiation time, and (b) degradation reaction kinetics on photocatalytic activity (10.0 mg/L SAM; 1.0 g/L dose of catalyst).
Photocatalytic degradation of SAM using Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 over 3 cycles (10.0 mg/L SAM; 1.0 g/L dose of catalyst) (a); XRD patterns of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 before and after 3 cycles (b).
Influence of several scavengers on photoactivity of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 (10.0 mg/L SAM, 4.0 h solar light, 1.0 g/L catalyst) (a); •OH-trapping PL spectra of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 (b).
Possible mechanism on Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system.

head.ce:title: Construction of novel Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 system with enhanced electron transfer capacity for visible light photocatalytic degradation of sulfanilamide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment Science, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Engineering and Science Program, University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH, 45221-0012, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Possible mechanism on Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system.

A novel Z-scheme system, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 with enhanced electron transfer capacity was constructed for degrading sulfanilamide (SAM) using solar light. The photocatalytic activity of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 was investigated. The effects of the mass ratio (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4), doped V dose, Ag wt.% content, and irradiation time on the catalytic performance were evaluated. The reasonable mechanism of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 solar photocatalytic degradation was also presented. These results reveal Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 possesses enhanced photocatalytic performance. The loaded Ag as electron mediator increases the electron transfer rate. Particularly, the doped V and the Fe ions from ZnFe2O4 form a powerful electron driving force, which enhances the electron transfer capacity. Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 shows optimal photocatalytic performance at 2.0 wt.% Ag and 0.5% doped V dose (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4 = 1.0:0.5). Also, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 exhibits high stability and repeatability in photocatalytic degradation. Several active species (•OH, •O2 −, and h +) are produced in the Z-scheme photodegradation of SAM. These results on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 are ascribed to synergistic photocatalytic effects of ZnFe2O4 and BiTa1-xVxO4 mediated through Ag and driven by doped V and Fe ions. Therefore, the Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic technology proves to be promising for the solar photocatalytic treatment of antibiotics under solar light.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamides are one class of antibiotics frequently used for human and veterinary medicine, and in agriculture as herbicides [1]. Sulfanilamide (SAM) has relatively stable chemical structure and composition because of the aromatic ring, heteroatoms, and other functional groups (see Fig. S1) [2]. Being persistent, SAM is hard to be degraded fully in the human body, and thus can trigger the formation of antibiotic resistance genes and antibiotic resistant bacteria [3], which may cause potential harm to human body and water ecology [4]. In recent years, there have been some reported technologies such as adsorption, flocculation and biodegradation in treating SAM in water. However, their application is limited due to some deficiencies such as long cycle, high cost, incomplete degradation and secondary pollution [5].

Nowadays, photocatalytic oxidation is a promising advanced oxidation technology in treating refractory organic pollutants in water/wastewater and solving energy shortage problems because it is effective, economic and environmental friendly [6–8]. Most of the wide band gap semiconductors that are used as photocatalysts to drive photocatalytic reactions can be excited only by ultraviolet (UV) which takes up 4–5% solar light [9]. To enhance sunlight use ratio, it is imperative to modify the wide band gap semiconductor photocatalysts to broaden their light response ranges, and develop efficient and practical solar light-driven photocatalytic systems for treating organic pollutants in water and wastewater.

Recently, Z-scheme photocatalyst has generated significant research attention due to its enhanced photocatalytic activity since it can effectively separate photogenerated electrons (e −) and photogenerated holes (h +) [10–12]. Usually, Z-scheme photocatalyst comprises two semiconductors. One is reductive photosystem I (PS I), and the other is oxidative photosystem II (PS II). The two semiconductors can absorb the solar light corresponding to their band gaps and are excited to produce electrons (e −) and holes (e −) pairs. In the Z-scheme photocatalyst, the photogenerated e − can migrate from conduction band (CB) from PS II to valence band (VB) of PS I, and combine with the photogenerated h + of PS I [9]. Hence, the e − of the CB from PS I and the h + of the VB from PS II can be effectively separated. The remaining h + from PS II show strong oxidizability, while residual e- from PS I display strong reducibility, which can obviously increase the photocatalytic activity.

As photocatalytic semiconductor material, BiTaO4 (E CB= -0.44 eV, E VB = 2.68 eV, E bg = 3.12 eV) has unique layered structure, superior band structure and excellent photostability [13]. It also has positive valence band (VB) with strong oxidation ability and can effectively degrade organic pollutants. ZnFe2O4 (E CB= -1.54 eV, E VB = 0.38 eV, E bg = 1.92 eV) has especial magnetic behavior, advantageous visible light response, and good photochemical stability [14]. It possesses negative conduction band (CB) with strong reducibility. What is important is that the potential of CB of BiTaO4 is close to the potential of VB of ZnFe2O4, which can form an ideal Z-scheme photocatalytic system. In view of the formation theory of Z-scheme photocatalyst, BiTaO4 with wide band gap and ZnFe2O4 with narrowband gap are selected to combine with each other to construct a Z-scheme photocatalyst [15,16]. ZnFe2O4 is used as the PS I, and BiTaO4 is used as the PS II. Thus, Z-scheme ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 photocatalyst with stronger oxidation-reduction capacity and wider light response range can be formed due to different band gaps of the two semiconductors [17–19].

Moreover, it’s worth noting that on this basis, in the Z-scheme photocatalysts, precious metal Ag can be used as solid-state electron mediator [9], and doping V ion can broaden the light response range [20]. In this study, for the Z-scheme ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 photocatalyst, Ag is introduced as the electron mediator, and metal V is adopted to be doped into BiTaO4 (BiTa1-xVxO4). The loading of Ag can help to transfer the photogenerated e − of the CB of BiTaO4, and integrate with the h + of the VB of ZnFe2O4. As co-catalyst, Ag can also trap the e- of the CB of ZnFe2O4 [21]. Therefore, the electron transmission rate increases, and the photocatalytic performance of the Z-scheme photocatalyst is enhanced under visible light [9,10].

In particular, in the Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalyst, the existence of both doped V5+ ions and Fe3+ ions from the ZnFe2O4 can form a powerful electron driving force on both sides of the Ag due to the various valence states of V and Fe ions. The generated Fe4+ and V4+ have strong oxidation and reduction capacity, respectively. In this way, Fe4+ and V4+ easily undergo rapid redox reaction, turning back to original Fe3+ and V5+. Because of powerful electron driving force, the photogenerated e − on the CB of BiTa1-xVxO4 can recombine with h + on the VB of ZnFe2O4. Hence, the separating efficiency and oxidation-reduction capacity of photogenerated e--h + can be effectively improved due to the enhanced electron transfer capacity by doped V ion and Fe ion [22]. Besides, doping V5+ ion can decrease the band gap of BiTaO4, and expand the light absorption scope, favoring its absorption of the solar light with lower energy [18]. This Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system is expected to with enhanced electron transfer capacity be more efficient to destroy the organic contaminants in wastewater using solar power.

In this work, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite was constructed, and employed in the solar-responsive photocatalytic decomposition of sulfanilamide (SAM), as a targeted contaminant. The photocatalytic performance of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite was investigated under solar light illumination. The influences of the mass ratio (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4), doped V dose, Ag wt.% content and irradiation time on the catalytic performance were evaluated. Furthermore, the possible mechanism on Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system was proposed. The results show that the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite as the Z-scheme photocatalytic system with enhanced electron transfer capacity possesses excellent photocatalytic activity for decomposing SAM under solar light irradiation.

BiTa1-xVxO4 was fabricated by wet-chemical technique [18]. In a typical synthetic process, 8.0 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 8.0 mmol TaCl5 were each dissolved in two separate conical flasks with cover containing 50.0 mL ethanol under continuous magnetic stirring for 0.5 h to obtain solutions A and B. The A and B solutions were mixed with the addition of 0.5 mL nitric acid (HNO3). After stirring 0.5 h, a transparent precursor solution was aged for 24.0 h, and dried at 60 °C in a drying box for removal of ethanol. The sample was triturated, and then calcined at 800 °C for 3.0 h. BiTaO4 nanoparticles were obtained.

Similarly, BiTa1-xVxO4 (x = 0.1%, 0.3% and 0.5%) was synthesized as follows. Typically, another solution A was prepared in accordance with the above method. A total amount of 8.0 mmol tantalum chloride (TaCl5) and vanadium chloride (VCl3) at different molar ratios were dissolved in 50.0 mL ethanol under continuous stirring for 0.5 h, and solution C is acquired. The solution A and C were put together with the addition of 0.5 mL nitric acid (HNO3). Afterwards, the solution was stirred, dried, and calcined as described in the synthesis of BiTaO4 nanoparticles. Finally, BiTa1-xVxO4 (x = 0.1%, 0.3%, 0.5%) nanoparticles were obtained.

ZnFe2O4 was fabricated by microwave hydrothermal method. Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (9.0 mmol) and ZnCl2 (4.5 mmol) were added into 30.0 mL H2O under constant stirring for 10.0 min. NaOH was used for adjusting the pH of the mixed solution at 10.0−13.0. Afterwards, the solution was enclosed in a Teflon reactor, and put on a turntable in a microwave synthesizer (XH-800 G, Beijing XiangHu Science and Technology Development Co.LTD, China) for well-distributed heating. The mixed solution in the Teflon reactor was treated at 1.5 MPa for 0.5 h. The obtained sediments were multi-washed, dried, and ground to powder. Finally, ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles were obtained.

Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposites were prepared using the obtained ZnFe2O4 and BiTa1-xVxO4 nanoparticles through a UV photoreduction method [17,23], as shown in Fig. 1 . First, 1.0 g ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles and 0.02˜0.06 g AgNO3 were dissolved in 50.0 mL deionized water. A mercury lamp (500 W) was adopted to illuminate the soliquoid for 20.0 min to produce Ag/ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles. Afterwards, 1.0 g BiTa1-xVxO4 (x = 0.1%, 0.3% and 0.5%) was put into the soliquoid including Ag/ZnFe2O4, and ultrasonicated for 5.0 min. Then, the obtained product was centrifuged, dried, and calcined at 450 °C for 1.0 h in a muffle roaster. Finally, the sample was triturated, and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 composite nanoparticles were acquired.

The obtained nanoparticles were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Platinum Instruments Co. Ltd, Germany), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Carl Zeiss, MERLIN Co, Germany) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI Co. Ltd, USA), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, JEOL), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, XSAM800, Shimadzu-Kratos, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet Avatar 330, Nicolet Co. Ltd, USA), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, U-3900, Shimadzu, Co. Ltd, Japan), and Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, F-7000, Hitachi, Co. Ltd, Japan).

The assessment on the catalytic activities of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposites was conducted through visible light photocatalytic degradation of sulfanilamide (SAM). Typically, 25.0 mL SAM solution (10.0 mg/L) and 25.0 mg nanocomposite were mixed in a reactor, and stirred with no light for 30.0 min. Then, adsorption-desorption equilibrium is developed. Afterwards, a Xenon lamp (500 W, 199.3 W/m2) with <400 nm cut-off filters is used to illuminate the mixed solution, in LY-GHX-V photo reaction apparatus (Shanghai Lanyi Industria Co. Ltd, China), being kept at 10 °C with water coolant. At a specified time of 1.0 h, 1.0 mL SAM solution was taken out through 0.45 μm filter membrane, and tested by employing a Pro 210 HPLC (Agilent Company, USA). A C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5.0 μm) is adopted with CH3OH and H2O (V/V = 40/60) (Milli-Q, 18.2 MΩ cm) as mobile phase. The UV detector was set at 258 nm, and 20 μL sample load were used [24]. The SAM solution treated under visible light alone with no catalysts was also adopted as control. Also, those generated anions during the degradation of SAM were measured through Ion chromatography (ICS 90, Dionex Corporation, USA) using 9.0 mmol/L Na2CO3 as eluent with 1.0 mL/min flow rate [25]. Furthermore, the stability of photocatalysts was determined via testing for several reuse cycle. After each cycle use of the catalyst, it was recovered by washing and boiling using de-ionized water to remove residuals over catalysts, and dried at 105 °C.

Besides, the production of some reactive species including •OH, h+ and •O2 −, was also detected using isopropylalcohol (IPA), disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and benzoquinone (BQ), being as quenchers, respectively. This experiment was the same as the above photocatalytic degradation process but adding scavengers. IPA, BQ, and EDTA solutions of 1.0 mmol/L were used. Further, the hydroxyl radical (•OH) was examined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Concretely, 0.05 g catalyst was put in 50.0 mL of 5.0 × 10-4 mol/L terephthalic acid (TA) dissolved in NaOH solution (2.0 × 10-3 mol/L). The suspension was illuminated. At a specified time of 1.0 h, the sample solutions were taken out and centrifuged. The intensities were recorded [26].

Fig. 2 shows XRD pattern of ZnFe2O4, BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4, and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4. From Fig. 2(a), the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 29.7°, 34.9°, 42.9°, 53.1°, 56.2°, and 61.7° are attributed to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystal planes of ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles (JCPDS No. 22-1012) [21]. From Fig. 2(b), some obvious XRD peaks appear at 14.8°, 25.1°, 27.4°, 28.9°, 29.6°, 29.9°, 31.7°, 34.0°, 36.6°, 45.0° and 49.1°, belonging to the (101), (200), (211), (012), (210), (-201), (020), (212), (202), (104) and (400) planes, which are indexed to the phase of BiTaO4 nanoparticles (JCPDS No.16-0906) [18,27]. In comparison to the pure BiTaO4 in Fig. 2(b), the height of all the peaks of BiTa1-xVxO4 in Fig. 2(c) decrease due to adding V5+ [13,28]. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the major spectra peaks attributed to ZnFe2O4 and BiTa1-xVxO4 of ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite are clearly presented. Besides, the extra peaks at 37.6°, 44.0° and 63.8° correspond to (111), (200) and (220) crystal facet of Ag (JCPDS No. 65-2871) [9].

Additionally, the average crystal sizes of ZnFe2O4 and BiTaO4 nanoparticles were determined by means of the XRD data and the Debye-Scherrer equation to be in the ranges of 6.0–7.0 nm and 15.0–18.0 nm, respectively, as shown in Table 1 [29]. The crystal size of BiTa1-xVxO4 is demonstrated to be smaller than that of BiTaO4 due to the doping of a small amount of V5+, and the crystallite sizes of ZnFe2O4 and BiTa1-xVxO4 in the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 composite have no significant change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM, EDX, and TEM of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of as-received ZnFe2O4, BiTaO4, and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanoparticles can be observed in the SEM micrographs shown in Fig. 3 . From Fig. 3(a), ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles primarily form uniform sphere-like agglomeration morphology with 10.0–25.0 nm grain sizes [30]. In Fig. 3(b), BiTaO4 nanoparticles show irregular agglomeration morphology with the diameter of 30.0–70.0 nm [31]. Fig. 3(c) reveals that small-sized ZnFe2O4 particles are evenly distributed on BiTa1-xVxO4 nanoparticles. Noticeably, their shape has no obvious change in spite of the concomitance of ZnFe2O4 and BiTa1-xVxO4. Besides, Fig. 4 shows the EDX spectra of the synthesized Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4. The peaks of Zn, Fe, Bi, Ta, V, O and Ag elements are present, demonstrating that Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanoparticle is formed successfully.

Also, the morphology of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposites was characterized through TEM and HRTEM in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5(a) presents the agglomerated particles of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 with particle sizes of 16.0–25.0 nm. Small-sized ZnFe2O4 sphere particles, and BiTa1-xVxO4 with bigger size particles can be observed, as shown in the red zone of Fig. 5(a, c), which is identical to the SEM analysis. It is also found that some very small sphere particles distribute around larger particles, which testifies that Ag particles have successfully been deposited on the surface of ZnFe2O4. Moreover, Fig. 5(b, d) displays three distinct lattice fringes of 0.255 nm, 0.308 nm, and 0.236 nm, which correspond to (311) crystal lattice facet of ZnFe2O4, (-201) facet of BiTa1-xVxO4, and (111) crystal facet of Ag, respectively [17,32,13]. Based on the above analysis, the very small Ag particle can be determined to be located between BiTa1-xVxO4 and ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles of the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalyst.

FT-IR spectrum of ZnFe2O4, BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4, and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 can be observed in Fig. 6 . The wide peaks at 3452−3425 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of H2O and OH− groups on photocatalysts, while sharp peaks at 1633−1611 cm−1 are assigned to bending vibration of H2O and OH− [3]. Fig. 6(a) displays the characteristic band at 558.15 cm−1 being derived from the stretching vibration of the Fe-O bond of ZnFe2O4 [33]. From Fig. 6(b), the vibration peaks at 1000−400 cm−1 of the Bi-O and Ta-O of the BiTaO4 are also observed [31]. In Fig. 6(c), except for the Bi-O and Ta-O vibrations, additional symmetric stretching vibration of the VO of BiTa1-xVxO4 at 727 cm-1 is also shown [34]. For the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 in Fig. 6(d), the absorption peaks of Fe-O, Bi-O, Ta-O and VO bonds are observed at 1000−400 cm−1. In comparison with BiTaO4 and ZnFe2O4, there are no clear differences of the absorption peaks in Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 in Fig. 6(d). It reveals that the load of Ag at low content and the doping of V5+ ions at low content do not remarkably change the chemical structure of ZnFe2O4 and BiTaO4.

Elementary composition and valence bond structure of the as-prepared Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 is characterized by XPS spectrum (see Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7(a) indicates the main elements are Zn, Fe, Bi, Ta, V, Ag and O. As seen in Fig. 7(b), the peaks at 1044.4 eV (Zn 2p1/2) and 1021.16 eV (Zn 2p3/2) can confirm the existence of Zn2+ [35]. As seen in Fig. 7(c), the peaks at 724.8 eV and 711.27 eV are ascribed to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, which indicates that Fe exists at the oxidation state of Fe3+ [21] Fig. 7(d) shows binding energy peaks at 165.21 eV for Bi 4f5/2 and at 159.53 eV for Bi 4f7/2, suggesting that Bi exists at the chemical state of Bi3+ [13]. From Fig. 7(e, f), the characteristic peaks of Ta 26.18 eV (4f) and V 515.7 eV (2p) can be observed, respectively [36]. The XPS signals of O 1 s are observed at binding energies of 532.2 eV and 530.18 eV (Fig. 7(h)). As shown in Fig. 7(g), the peaks at 368.2 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 374.2 eV (Ag 3d3/2) can be detected which supports the presence of metallic Ag (Ag°) [37]. Therefore, the above results prove that Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 has been successfully obtained.

The UV–vis DRS of ZnFe2O4, BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4, and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 can be seen in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8(a), ZnFe2O4 shows strong absorption in the UV–vis region. BiTaO4 exhibits strong absorption of UV–vis scope, and steep absorption edges is observed [13,38]. But the edge of absorption band of BiTa1-xVxO4 reveals red-shift, which is basically ascribed to the doping of V5+ ion. Also, compared with ZnFe2O4 and BiTa1-xVxO4, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 shows shift of absorption edge to a longer wavelength of visible light scope and the increase of absorption intensity, which results from the interaction of BiTa1-xVxO4 and ZnFe2O4, and the introduction of Ag particles. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 8(b), the optical band gap values of BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4, ZnFe2O4 and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 are 3.12 eV, 3.0 eV, 1.92 eV and 1.87 eV, respectively, which is estimated by the formula as follows [9]: Eg (eV) = 1240/λ, in which λ□ is the maximum absorption wavelength. It indicates that the band gap of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 becomes narrower and is conducive to absorbing the light in a wider range.

In the photocatalytic system, the trap, migration and recombination of charge carriers can be analyzed by PL spectra. Generally, low PL signal means high separation efficiency of photogenerated e −-h +, and consequently high photocatalytic activity of semiconductors [3]. PL spectra of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4, BiTa1-xVxO4, BiTaO4 and ZnFe2O4 are shown in Fig. 9 . BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4 and ZnFe2O4 show strong emission intensity, revealing that the photogenerated electrons (e −) and photogenerated holes (h +) recombine rapidly. But Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 shows lower emission peaks than BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4 and ZnFe2O4, indicating that the recombination of photogenerated e − and photogenerated h + is suppressed over Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite, and the photocatalytic activity is enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of mass ratio of ZnFe2O4 and BiTaO4 on photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 10 , as the mass ratio of ZnFe2O4 and BiTaO4 (ZnFe2O4: BiTaO4) changes at the scope of 1.0:2.0–1.0:0.5, the extent of the degradation of SAM rises. The degradation extent reaches 51.9% upon visible light irradiation for 4.0 h when the mass ratio is 1.0:0.5 (ZnFe2O4: BiTaO4).

The results demonstrate the photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 is higher when adopting the mass ratio of 1.0:0.5 (ZnFe2O4: BiTaO4), which relies on the capacity of BiTaO4 producing photogenerated e − and VB of ZnFe2O4 accepting the e − [39]. At the mass ratio of 1.0:0.5, the ZnFe2O4 accepts more photo-induced e − of BiTaO4 (PS II), which is beneficial for ZnFe2O4 and BiTaO4 to form Z-scheme system ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 interfaces. Hence, to a certain degree, the photogenerated e − and photogenerated h + is separated, decreasing the recombination [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of dose of doped V on photocatalytic activity of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 11 , the extent of the degradation gradually increases with increasing the dose of added V in ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 (x = 0.0−0.5 %), but decreases at the dose (x = 0.6%). Upon illumination, only 44.9% SAM was removed within 4.0 h using ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 with no V doped, while 55.7%, 59.7% and 62.9% SAM was degraded using ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 at x = 0.1%, 0.3% and 0.5%, respectively. The results reveal proper amount of doped V enhances the photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4. This results from that the doped V ion combining with the Fe ion from the ZnFe2O4 can form an electron driving force, promoting efficient transmission of electrons and also separation of charge carriers [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of wt.% of Ag on photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 and ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 12 , with increasing wt.% content of Ag in ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 nanocomposite, the photocatalytic degradation extent first increases at 0–2.0 % Ag, and decreases with raising Ag content from 2.0% to 3.0%. Using ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 with no Ag under visible light irradiation, only 44.5% SAM was removed within 4.0 h, while 52.7%, 57.1% and 48.4% SAM were degraded, respectively, when 1.0%, 2.0%, and 3.0% Ag were adopted in Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTaO4 nanocomposite. Also, from Fig. 13 , as the Ag content added into ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 increases, the degradation extent increases at 0–2.0% Ag, and then decreases at 2.0–3.0%. Under visible light irradiation, only 51.9% SAM was degraded within 4.0 h for ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 with no Ag, while 69.7%, 83.8% and 68.2% degradation were obtained, respectively, for Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 with 1.0%, 2.0%, and 3.0% Ag. The results reveal the introduced Ag improves the degradation of SAM in solution, and enhances the photocatalytic activity of ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 and ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4. This is attributed to the role of Ag as electron transfer mediator accelerating the electron transmission, and consequently promoting the charge separation [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of irradiation time on photocatalytic performance of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfanilamide (SAM) solution shows the absorption peaks at 258 nm which are attributed to the benzene ring of SAM (See Fig. 14 ). Fig. 15 (a) demonstrates the influence of the irradiation time (0.0–6.0 h) on photocatalytic activity of catalysts such as BiTa1-xVxO4, ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4. The adsorption process of SAM was conducted with no light for 1.0 h for developing adsorption-desorption equilibrium. When this had been reached, 2.9%, 3.2%, 5.1% and 5.3% adsorption of SAM were obtained for ZnFe2O4, BiTa1-xVxO4, ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4, respectively, indicating only a small amount of SAM could be removed due to adsorption on the catalyst surface. Also, the extent of degradation of SAM achieved within 6.0 h under visible light alone was only 4.1%, which reveals that the photodegradation of SAM can be negligible without any catalysts. However, in the presence of the catalyst, with increasing irradiation time in the studied range of 0.0–6.0 h, the degradation extent gradually increased. For BiTa1-xVxO4, ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 and Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic systems, 35.5%, 38.3%, 62.1% and 100% degradation was obtained within 6.0 h light irradiation, respectively. Therefore, the order of adsorption of these catalysts is shown as follows: Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 > ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 > BiTa1-xVxO4 > ZnFe2O4, and the order of degradation of SAM using these catalysts is listed as follows: Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 > ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 > ZnFe2O4 > BiTa1-xVxO4. Further, Fig. 15(b) displays the pseudo-first-order kinetics profiles of the photocatalytic degradation reactions, and -ln (C/C0) = kapp t, in which t means the irradiation time and kapp means the reaction rate constant [3]. It is concluded that kapp are 0.601, 0.153, 0.104 and 0.0732 min−1, for the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4, ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4, ZnFe2O4, and BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system, respectively. By comparison, the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system has the maximum kapp for the photocatalytic degradation reaction. The order of kapp of the reaction kinetics is listed as follows: kapp (Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4) > kapp (ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4) > kapp (ZnFe2O4) > kapp (BiTa1-xVxO4) > kapp (light alone).

Besides, the analysis on ion chromatography (IC) of SAM solution is presented in Fig. S2. The NO3 − and SO4 2− peaks are shown at 6.9 and 10.6 min retention time, respectively. It indicates that the deamination reaction of the aromatic ring of SAM molecule and the subsequent breaking of the -SO2NH2 group into SO4 2− ion, leads to the oxidation of initial NH4 + into NO3- ions during the degradation [42]. By contrast, the peak areas for Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 system are more than those for ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4 and ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 systems. Also, with increase in irradiation time (4.0–6.0 h), the ion peak areas increase for the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 system. The ion peak areas reach the maximum within 6.0 h visible light irradiation. These results indicate that SAM can be degraded rapidly and completely within 6.0 h in the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system, and the photocatalytic activity of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 is higher than the other catalyst samples. The photocatalytic performance order of catalysts is shown as follows: Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 > ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4 > ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4. The results demonstrate that Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 exhibits better photocatalytic performance, and the addition of Ag and the doping of V ion improve the photocatalytic performance of catalysts.

Photostability and recyclability of catalysts are significant for the actual application in treating pollutants in wastewater [40]. In the current study, stability and recycle use of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite were evaluated via reuse tests. Fig. 16 (a) shows that the removal efficiency of SAM decreases from 100% for the 1 st cycle to 96.4% for the 2nd cycle, and to 92.9% for the 3rd cycle, within 6.0 h, adopting 1.0 g/L photocatalyst for each run. Additionally, Fig. 16(b) displays the XRD of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite before and after the degradation reactions of SAM, suggesting that the crystal phase of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 remains unchanged after three consecutive cycles. This slight reduction of degradation efficiency after three cycles may derive from the slight loss of the photocatalyst during the centrifugation and washing steps. Therefore, the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite has superior stability and reusability.

In this study, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and benzoquinone (BQ) can be adopted as quenchers to analyze the production of hydroxyl radicals (
OH), holes (h +) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −), respectively [43]. Fig. 17 (a) displays that 83.8% SAM is degraded within 4.0 h with no scavenger, while the degradation extent decreases to 49.8%, 19.6% and 56.0%, respectively, when adding IPA, BQ and EDTA. This decrease in %removal of SAM results from the consumption of
OH,
O2 − and h + by the scavengers IPA, BQ and EDTA, respectively, indicating the
OH,
O2 − and h + active species are produced in Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic degradation system [44]. Besides, the %degradation of SAM in the presence of BQ is the lowest, which suggests the production and consumption of much of
O2 −. Although the
O2 − is weak, it can oxidize H2O and further generate
OH via some redox reactions [45].

Also, terephthalic acid (TA) was adopted to be a probe for the determination of the production of
OH over the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 using photoluminescence (PL) technology [46]. When TA reacts with
OH in solution, the characteristic fluorescence at 425 nm (λex = 315 nm) of the 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) is received. From Fig. 17(b), the intensity rises with the irradiation time increasing from 0 h to 4.0 h, which indicates the
OH radicals are generated in Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system, and the amount of
OH increases with increasing irradiation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 18 reveals the possible mechanism on solar-light-driven Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 Z-scheme photocatalyst with enhanced electron transport capacity for degrading SAM in aqueous solution. From the UV–vis DRS in Fig. 8, the band-gap of BiTaO4, BiTa1-xVxO4 and ZnFe2O4 are estimated to be 3.12 eV, 3.0 eV and 1.92 eV, respectively. By calculation, E CB and E VB of BiTa1-xVxO4 are 0.29 eV and 3.29 eV, respectively [47]. In addition, BiTa1-xVxO4 (E CB = 0.29 eV, E VB = 3.29 eV) alone has very weak reduction ability, so the production of •O2 − is unfavorable. Nevertheless, BiTa1-xVxO4 has more positive VB, and ZnFe2O4 (E CB= -1.54 eV, E VB = 0.38 eV) has more negative CB. The CB of BiTa1-xVxO4 is also very close to the VB of ZnFe2O4, which is suitable for constructing an ideal Z-scheme photocatalyst. The results on the trapping experiments (in Section 3.12) also certify the production of
O2 − (see Fig. 17(a)), suggesting Z-scheme photocatalyst being formed.

In the Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 system, ZnFe2O4 (E bg = 1.92 eV) and BiTa1-xVxO4 (E bg = 3.0 eV) act as PS I and PS II, respectively, and the loaded Ag is adopted as electron transfer channel. Under solar light illumination, both BiTa1-xVxO4 and ZnFe2O4 are excited to generate electrons and holes. The photo-generated e − of the CB of BiTa1-xVxO4 (PS II) can transfer rapidly to the VB of ZnFe2O4 through part of the Ag (ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4), and combined with the holes (h +) from ZnFe2O4. It promotes effective separation of e − of the CB of ZnFe2O4 and the h + of the VB of BiTa1-xVxO4 [48]. As co-catalyst, the other part of the Ag on the surface of ZnFe2O4 (Ag/ZnFe2O4) can also trap e − of the CB of ZnFe2O4, which helps improve the separation of photoinduced carriers. The cumulative photoinduced e − in Ag easily reacts with O2 to get
O2- that can also oxidize H2O to produce
OH via some chemical reactions [9]. The remaining h + of VB of BiTa1-xVxO4 can oxidize H2O to yield
OH radicals. Thus, those active
OH, h + and
O2- can oxidize and degrade SAM in aqueous solution.

Here, it is noteworthy that according to the previous studies [20], the doped V5+ can be reduced to V4+ by the photogenerated e− of the CB of BiTa1-xVxO4, while the Fe3+ on ZnFe2O4 can be oxidized to Fe4+ by the photogenerated h+ of the VB of ZnFe2O4. Thus, a powerful electron driving force can be developed on both sides of Ag nanoparticles due to the generated Fe4+ with strong electron accepting ability and the generated V4+ with strong the electron donating ability. The presence of the Fe4+ with strong oxidation capacity and the V4+ with strong reduction capacity promotes the rapid transfer of e- of the conduction band of BiTa1-xVxO4 to the valence band of ZnFe2O4, and the combination with the h+ of ZnFe2O4 [3]. It further leads to the complete separation of the e- of the CB of ZnFe2O4 and the h + of the VB of BiTa1-xVxO4 to realize high-efficiency solar photocatalytic degradation of SAM.

Also, the band-gap of BiTaO4 (3.12 eV) decreases to 3.0 eV (BiTa1-xVxO4) due to doping of V5+ ion into the BiTaO4, and broadens the light response range. Being a rational Z-scheme photocatalyst, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 system with enhanced electron transfer capacity displays superior photocatalytic performance caused by the doping of V ion and the load of Ag particle [20,23].

Detailed photocatalytic reactions may be as follows.
(1) Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 + hν → h + + e − + Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 *
(2) O2 + e − →
O2 −
(3) OH− + h + →
OH
(4) V5+ (BiTa1-xVxO4) + e − → V4+ (BiTa1-xVxO4)
(5) Fe3+ (ZnFe2O4) + h + → Fe4+ (ZnFe2O4)
(6) V4+ (BiTa1-xVxO4) + Fe4+ (ZnFe2O4) → V5+ (BiTa1-xVxO4) + Fe3+ (ZnFe2O4)

Novel Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 nanocomposite was synthesized using wet-chemical method and microwave hydrothermal method, and was evaluated for visible light photocatalytic oxidation of SAM in water. Combination of BiTaO4 and ZnFe2O4 can form an ideal Z-scheme photocatalyst. The photocatalytic activity of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 is higher than those of ZnFe2O4, BiTa1-xVxO4, ZnFe2O4/BiTaO4, and ZnFe2O4/BiTa1-xVxO4. The loaded Ag as electron mediator can promote the electron transfer. Particularly, the doped V and the Fe ions from ZnFe2O4 can form a powerful electron driving force, which promotes the transfer of the e− from the CB of BiTa1-xVxO4 to the VB of ZnFe2O4. The doping of V5+ ion is beneficial to broadening the range of light response. Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 shows optimal photocatalytic performance at 2.0 wt.% Ag and 0.5% doped V dose (ZnFe2O4:BiTaO4 = 1.0:0.5). The photocatalytic degradation of SAM reaches 100% using Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 within 6.0 h. Also, Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 has superior photocatalytic stability and reusability. Moreover, active species (•OH, h +, and •O2 −) are produced during the photocatalytic reaction in the presence of Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4. Therefore, the Z-scheme Ag/ZnFe2O4/Ag/BiTa1-xVxO4 photocatalytic system with enhanced electron transfer capacity may provide a promising technology for efficiently dealing with antibiotics in wastewater using solar power.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41771503), the support from the Innovative Team Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province (LT2018018) of China, the Key Laboratory Project of Shenyang Municipal Science and Technology Plan (19-109-1-003) of China. D. D. Dionysiou also thanks financial support from the University of Cincinnati through a UNESCO co-Chair Professor position on “Water Access and Sustainability” and the Herman Schneider Professorship in the College of Engineering and Applied Sciences. The authors also thank Dr. Jie Yang from McGill University of Canada and School of Education of Shenyang Normal University of China for her contribution to this work.
